sublevels.theory:
No real-world C program is entirely written inside one massive `main.c` file. Code is split aggressively into multiple files for readability and reusability.

**Header Files (`.h`)**
These contain the *Declarations* (Function Prototypes, Struct definitions, Macros, Extern variables). You use `#include "math_utils.h"` to import these into your other files.

**Source Files (`.c`)**
These contain the actual executable *Definitions* (the `{ body }` of the functions). 

If `main.c` uses an `add()` function defined physically inside `math_utils.c`, how do they connect? 
You compile them simultaneously!
`gcc main.c math_utils.c -o myprogram.exe`
The Linker merges them at the very end.

questions.question: Why do programmers split C projects into multiple files?
options: For modularity, code reusability, and readability, preventing a single massive file
Because GCC restricts files to 1000 lines
questions.answer: For modularity, code reusability, and readability, preventing a single massive file

questions.question: What specifically should standard `.h` Header Files contain?
options: Declarations (Prototypes, Structs, Macros) acting as a 'Table of Contents' for other files
The actual executable logic and loops of functions
questions.answer: Declarations (Prototypes, Structs, Macros) acting as a 'Table of Contents' for other files

questions.question: What specifically should `.c` Source Files contain?
options: The actual Executable Function Definitions (the `{ ... body }` logic)
questions.answer: The actual Executable Function Definitions (the `{ ... body }` logic)

questions.question: If `main.c` needs to use a function declared in `math.h`, what must `main.c` do?
options: Use `#include "math.h"` at the top of the file
questions.answer: Use `#include "math.h"` at the top of the file

questions.question: How do you correctly compile a program that is split across `main.c` and `helper.c`?
questions.answer: gcc main.c helper.c -o output

questions.question: Which phase of compilation actually "connects" the function call in `main.c` to the function logic located physically inside `helper.c`?
questions.answer: Linking

questions.question: What error do you get if you compile `main.c` but forget to compile/link `helper.c` alongside it?
options: Undefined Reference / Unresolved External Symbol (Linker Error)
questions.answer: Undefined Reference / Unresolved External Symbol (Linker Error)

questions.question: Should you ever put `#include "helper.c"` inside your `main.c` file?
options: No! Standard C practice is to only #include `.h` files, NEVER physically #include `.c` source files.
questions.answer: No! Standard C practice is to only #include `.h` files, NEVER physically #include `.c` source files.

questions.question: If multiple C files need to share the same exact Global Variable, what keyword must be used in the header file declaration?
questions.answer: extern

questions.question: What happens if you define the `{ body }` of a function inside a Header file `math.h`, and you include that header in 3 different `.c` files?
options: Multiple Definition Linker Error; the actual code will physically exist 3 times, causing the Linker to crash when merging
questions.answer: Multiple Definition Linker Error; the actual code will physically exist 3 times, causing the Linker to crash when merging

sublevels.theory:
If you have 50 `.c` files, typing `gcc file1.c file2.c ... file50.c -o app` every time you make a tiny edit is insane. What's worse, GCC will completely recompile all 50 files from scratch, literally taking minutes!

**Object Files (`.o` or `.obj`)**
Instead of compiling straight to `.exe`, you use `gcc -c main.c` to create `main.o` (an unconnected, compiled binary puzzle piece of just that one file). 

**Makefiles (`make`)**
A Makefile is a script that tracks dependencies. If you only edit `math_utils.c`, `make` is smart enough to ONLY recompile `math_utils.c` into `math_utils.o`, and then simply re-link it with the existing `main.o` and other unchanged files. It turns a 2-minute build into 1 second!

questions.question: What is an Object file (`.o` or `.obj`)?
options: An un-linked, compiled binary 'puzzle piece' of a single C source file
questions.answer: An un-linked, compiled binary 'puzzle piece' of a single C source file

questions.question: What compiler flag creates an Object file without attempting to invoke the Linker?
questions.answer: -c

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of Object Files in giant projects?
options: If one C file changes, you only recompile that single Object file; you don't have to recompile the other 99 unchanged C files
questions.answer: If one C file changes, you only recompile that single Object file; you don't have to recompile the other 99 unchanged C files

questions.question: What does a `Makefile` conceptually do?
options: Tracks file dependencies and intelligently automates the compilation of only the files that changed since the last build
questions.answer: Tracks file dependencies and intelligently automates the compilation of only the files that changed since the last build

questions.question: What is the syntax format of a Makefile rule?
questions.answer: target: dependencies \n \t command

questions.question: What crucially specific invisible character MUST precede the compilation command inside a Makefile rule?
questions.answer: A Tab character

questions.question: If you just type `make` in the terminal, what does it do by default?
options: Executes the very first target rule defined at the top of the Makefile
questions.answer: Executes the very first target rule defined at the top of the Makefile

questions.question: What is typically the purpose of a `make clean` rule?
options: To delete the final executables and all `.o` object files so you can force a fresh, absolute rebuild
questions.answer: To delete the final executables and all `.o` object files so you can force a fresh, absolute rebuild

questions.question: In a Makefile, if `main.o: main.c math.h`, what happens if you edit `math.h`?
options: Make notices the timestamp of `math.h` is newer than `main.o`, so it destroys and rebuilds `main.o` automatically
questions.answer: Make notices the timestamp of `math.h` is newer than `main.o`, so it destroys and rebuilds `main.o` automatically

questions.question: What tool replaces standard Makefiles in the modern era to generate project configurations cross-platform seamlessly?
questions.answer: CMake

sublevels.theory:
**Static Libraries (`.a` / `.lib`)**
If you create a physics engine out of 20 `.o` object files, you can zip them all together using the `ar` (Archiver) tool into a single Static Library file (`libphysics.a`). 
When you compile your main program and link it against `libphysics.a`, the Linker literally extracts ONLY the exact functions you actually used, and physically pastes them inside your final `.exe`, making your `.exe` stand-alone!

**Dynamic/Shared Libraries (`.so` / `.dll`)**
Unlike Static linking, Dynamic Libraries are NOT pasted inside your `.exe`. Your `.exe` remains tiny. Instead, your program loads the DLL from your computer's hard drive into RAM *at runtime*. Multiple programs can share a single DLL in memory simultaneously!

questions.question: What is a Static Library (`.a`) fundamentally?
options: A bundled archive containing exclusively compiled `.o` object files
questions.answer: A bundled archive containing exclusively compiled `.o` object files

questions.question: What happens during Static Linking?
options: The linker extracts the specific required functions from the library and physically embeds their machine code directly inside your final `.exe`
The code links via the internet
questions.answer: The linker extracts the specific required functions from the library and physically embeds their machine code directly inside your final `.exe`

questions.question: What is a major advantage of Static Linking?
options: Your final executable is 100% physically self-contained and stand-alone (no "missing DLL" errors when sharing your app)
questions.answer: Your final executable is 100% physically self-contained and stand-alone (no "missing DLL" errors when sharing your app)

questions.question: What does a Dynamic / Shared Library (`.so` on Linux, `.dll` on Windows) do differently?
options: It is NOT embedded in the exe; the program searches for it on the hard drive and loads it dynamically into RAM while running
questions.answer: It is NOT embedded in the exe; the program searches for it on the hard drive and loads it dynamically into RAM while running

questions.question: What is a massive advantage of Dynamic Libraries?
options: If 10 running programs use the same DLL, the OS only loads ONE copy of it into RAM, massive saving system memory.
questions.answer: If 10 running programs use the same DLL, the OS only loads ONE copy of it into RAM, massive saving system memory.

questions.question: If you update a Dynamic Library (e.g. `libmath.dll`) with a critical bug fix, do you need to physically recompile your main `.exe` program?
options: No, because the program just loads whatever is currently in the DLL file at runtime; you only need to swap the DLL file! (Assuming signatures didn't change)
questions.answer: No, because the program just loads whatever is currently in the DLL file at runtime; you only need to swap the DLL file! (Assuming signatures didn't change)

questions.question: What command creates a static library archive from multiple object files?
questions.answer: ar rcs libname.a file1.o file2.o

questions.question: If you have a global variable declared in `fileA.c`, but you prepend it with `static` (e.g., `static int config_var = 1;`), what effect does this have on Multi-file Compilation?
options: It restricts the variable's "Linkage" so that it is physically totally invisible to all other files; no other file can 'extern' it.
It shares it with all files globally
questions.answer: It restricts the variable's "Linkage" so that it is physically totally invisible to all other files; no other file can 'extern' it.

questions.question: What happens if a Dynamic Library your program relies on is unexpectedly deleted from your computer?
options: The program crashes upon launching with a missing dependency / DLL Not Found error
It runs silently without that code
questions.answer: The program crashes upon launching with a missing dependency / DLL Not Found error

questions.question: Can you use `#include` to load a DLL?
options: No, `#include` is strictly for text-based Header files during compilation. To load a DLL at runtime manually you use `dlopen()` on Linux or `LoadLibrary()` on Windows.
questions.answer: No, `#include` is strictly for text-based Header files during compilation. To load a DLL at runtime manually you use `dlopen()` on Linux or `LoadLibrary()` on Windows.
